_comment: Strict JSON does not support real comments. This template uses _comment-style fields to guide the user.
template_purpose: Use this as a repo-grounded prompt framework for drafting implementation artifacts in AZQ.

role.instruction: You are drafting a repo-grounded implementation artifact for AZQ.

first_step.instruction: Inspect the current repository state before writing anything.

artifact_request._comment: User input required. Say exactly what file Codex should produce.
artifact_request._target_filename_comment: Be concrete. Include the exact filename, exact output type, and whether this is a drop-in replacement or a new file.

repo_inspection._comment: User input required. List the files and directories Codex must inspect before drafting.
repo_inspection.instruction: Inspect the repository before drafting. Match the reality on disk, not an imagined architecture.
read_first: <REQUIRED: adjacent wave files of the same type>
repo_inspection._read_first_comment: Useful instructions include: read the current manifest and checks files first, validate against live filenames and the current module layout, and do not assume the docs are perfectly current.

artifact_purpose._comment: User input required. Explain what the artifact is for so Codex has doctrine instead of vibes.
artifact_purpose.summary: <REQUIRED: what this artifact is trying to do>

primary_rule.instruction: Match the reality on disk, not an imagined architecture.

requirements._comment: User input required. These are hard constraints.
required: preserve the repo's existing style and vocabulary
keep the artifact aligned with the current runner and check format
ground checks in current public behavior where possible
use hard checks for breakage and human_checks for judgment
keep the wave and task intent narrow
requirements._required_comment: Separate hard constraints from preferences. Examples of good hard constraints: valid JSON, match current runner format, include all manifest task IDs, use baseline checks plus task-specific checks.

preferences._comment: User input optional. These are preferred behaviors, not hard requirements.
preferred: use behavior checks over grep checks
keep brittle architecture policing in human_checks
avoid exact implementation assumptions unless required

constraints._comment: These are explicit do-not rules. Add more as needed for the specific artifact.
do_not: do not invent missing modules unless the task explicitly requires them
do not assume docs are current if the code disagrees
do not silently rewrite adjacent files
do not overfit hard checks to one exact refactor shape when behavior checks are possible
do not broaden into future-stage behavior unless explicitly asked
constraints._do_not_comment: For Codex, do not is gold.

validation._comment: User input optional. Keep these checks aligned with the artifact type.
before_finishing: validate syntax for the target file type
check task IDs match the manifest when a manifest is involved
confirm referenced filenames actually exist or are explicitly planned by the task
note any brittle checks or underspecified assumptions

mismatch_handling._comment: Tell Codex to explain reality mismatches plainly.
instructions: if the manifest and the repo disagree, say so
if a check is underspecified, note it
if a hard check would be brittle, move it to human_checks and explain why

report_back._comment: Tell Codex what to summarize after writing the file.
summarize: any checks or judgments intentionally left in human_checks

prompt_fill_in_example._comment: Optional example showing the kind of concrete input that works well.
prompt_fill_in_example.artifact_purpose: Draft a Stage 2 Wave D checks recipe for a refactor-prep wave that preserves Stage 2 behavior without introducing Stage 3 behavior.

good_analog.instruction: Use this existing artifact as the closest model for decomposition and tone.

closest_existing_checks_analog.reason: Stage 3 Wave B is the Agenda command-layer analogue of Stage 2 Wave B.
